title: Naked Statistics
subtitle: Stripping the Dread from the Data
author: Charles Wheelan
excerpt: A witty, math-averse-friendly tour of the statistical ideas that actually shape the modern world — mean, median, standard deviation, correlation, probability, the central limit theorem, polling, inference, and regression — written by a former Economist correspondent to give any reader the tools to stop being fooled by numbers.
keyIdeas: Statistics is the detective work that turns raw data into a meaningful answer — every dataset is a crime scene.
Mean, median, and mode answer different questions; standard deviation tells you how much the data is spread out.
Correlation is not causation — the most abused phrase in all of data analysis.
The Central Limit Theorem is the wizard behind the curtain: large random samples reliably approximate the population.
The Monty Hall problem shows that human intuition about probability is systematically wrong.
Statistical significance is a flexible convention, not a fixed truth — p < 0.05 is a habit, not a law of nature.
Regression analysis is the best tool for finding patterns in complex data, but its misuse kills people.
A representative sample of roughly one thousand Americans can describe a country of three hundred million — sampling is the miracle that makes modern empirical work possible.
whoShouldRead: Anyone who has ever felt 'I'm not a math person' but wants to read research critically
Journalists, marketers, and managers who work with data but never had formal training
Students in introductory statistics or social-science methods courses
Citizens trying to evaluate polls, medical studies, and economic claims in the news
Educators who teach quantitative reasoning at the high school or undergraduate level
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for a formal statistics textbook with proofs and derivations
Professional statisticians who already know the material
Anyone who needs coverage of Bayesian methods, machine learning, or causal inference frameworks
relatedBooks: The Signal and the Noise by Nate Silver
How Not to Be Wrong by Jordan Ellenberg
Thinking, Fast and Slow by Daniel Kahneman
The Drunkard's Walk by Leonard Mlodinow
Superforecasting by Philip Tetlock and Dan Gardner
The Black Swan by Nassim Nicholas Taleb
